title: Why Deeper Isn't Automatically Better
concept: Adding layers can increase what a CNN could represent, but a plain deep stack may become much harder to optimize, so training can stall or even get worse as depth keeps growing.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a residual block, what are the learned layers encouraged to produce?

options.text: A useful adjustment that gets added to the input
A hard drive backup of the training set
A promise that deeper layers never fail

exercises.explain: Right — residual blocks matter because plain depth by itself can be surprisingly hard to train.

cards.title: More relays, more chances to muddle the message
cards.body: Passing a note through three people is usually manageable. Passing it through one hundred people is much riskier: the message can fade, distort, or arrive too late to be useful. A very deep plain CNN can face a similar problem during training.

cards.title: Depth adds power and optimization pain
cards.body: In theory, adding layers can let a network model more complex patterns. But in a plain stack, that extra depth can make optimization harder. Activations and gradients must travel through many transformations, and the training process can struggle enough that a deeper model even shows worse training accuracy than a shallower one. This is part of the intuition behind the degradation problem.

cards.title: Too many handoffs in a delivery chain
cards.body: A package moving through two warehouses may arrive smoothly. A package routed through fifty busy warehouses has more chances for delay, confusion, or damage. Depth can add useful stages, but every extra handoff also makes the route harder to manage.

cards.title: A deeper model trains worse
cards.body: Imagine a 20-layer plain CNN and a 100-layer plain CNN trained on the same image task. You might expect the 100-layer model to do at least as well, because it has more capacity. But in practice, the deeper plain model may optimize poorly and end up with higher training error, even before we worry about generalization.

cards.title: Bad training does not mean deeper features are useless
cards.body: A common mistake is to conclude that if a very deep plain model trains badly, then deeper representations must be pointless. Not necessarily. The issue may be that the architecture makes those layers hard to use and hard to train — not that deeper reasoning has no value.

cards.title: Reasonable depth vs. fragile plain depth
cards.body: Why 'more layers' is not a free win:

left.text:
Shorter path for activations and gradients.
Optimization is usually manageable.
Extra layers can still help.

right.text:
Long chain of transformations.
Signals can weaken or get distorted.
Training may become harder, not easier.

exercises.prompt: Why isn't adding more CNN layers automatically a win?

options.text: Because deeper plain stacks can become harder to optimize, so training can get worse instead of better
Because convolution stops working once a model has too many layers
Because deeper networks can only process grayscale images
Because extra layers delete the loss function

exercises.explain: Exactly. Depth can add capacity, but plain depth can also make optimization much more fragile.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a 100-layer plain CNN has worse training accuracy than a 20-layer one, the extra depth may be causing optimization trouble.
exercises.explain: True. When a deeper model does worse even on the training set, that often points to optimization difficulty rather than a lack of representational power.

exercises.sentence: In very deep plain stacks, gradients can become weak or unstable as depth ___ .
exercises.explain: Grows is right — the problem often gets worse as the path through the network becomes longer.

exercises.prompt: Which situation best matches the 'deeper isn't automatically better' idea?

options.text: A plain 80-layer CNN trains worse than a shallower version because the long chain is hard to optimize
A CNN gains a useful extra layer and trains slightly better
An image is stored as PNG instead of JPEG

exercises.explain: Right — the key issue is optimization pain in a very deep plain stack, not file format or the idea of depth itself.

exercises.prompt: A team trains a plain 24-layer CNN and a plain 80-layer CNN on the same dataset. The 80-layer model gets worse training accuracy, not just worse validation accuracy. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The extra plain depth likely made optimization harder, so the deeper model became more difficult to train well
More layers always reduce training accuracy by mathematical law
The shallow model must secretly be using skip connections even if none were added
Training accuracy never matters when comparing architectures

exercises.explain: Exactly. If the deeper plain model struggles even on the training set, the problem is often optimization difficulty through that long chain. Next, we'll see why skip connections are such an effective fix.
